metadata.career_relevance: Razorpay's GraphQL API powers mobile apps, web dashboards, and third-party integrations. A broken GraphQL resolver (wrong field name, missing auth check, N+1 bug) can silently break all these clients. GraphQlTester tests the complete GraphQL execution: schema parsing, resolver invocation, response serialization, error handling — just like a real client. After this topic, you can write tests that catch GraphQL-specific bugs that regular unit tests miss.

hook.real_world_problem:
Razorpay GraphQL API bug that MockMvc wouldn't catch:

```
Schema:
  type Query { merchant(id: String!): Merchant }
  type Merchant { id: String, name: String, payments: [Payment] }

Bug: MerchantResolver returns merchant.name in the 'id' field by mistake

MockMvc test: POST /graphql with {query: '{ merchant(id: "M1") { id } }'}
  → HTTP 200 OK
  → body contains JSON
  → test PASSES (MockMvc only checks HTTP layer!)

GraphQlTester test:
  .path("merchant.id").entity(String.class).isEqualTo("MRC-001")
  → FAILS: actual value is 'Flipkart' (the name was returned as id)
  → Bug caught before production!

GraphQL-specific bugs GraphQlTester catches:
  - Wrong field returned (name in id field)
  - Missing required field (null where not-null expected)
  - GraphQL error vs empty response
  - Auth not applied (private data returned)
  - N+1 not actually batched (checked via query count)
```
hook.what_you_will_build: Complete GraphQlTester test suite for Razorpay's payment and merchant GraphQL API.

visual_aid.description: Two paths: Test → GraphQlTester → Spring GraphQL execution engine (resolvers, schema validation, error handling) → Response assertions. Compare with: Test → MockMvc → HTTP layer (just checks status code + raw JSON). GraphQlTester goes deeper: validates GraphQL-specific semantics. Second part: @GraphQlTest components — only loads @Controller, Schema, and @MockBean services (not full Spring context). Faster than @SpringBootTest.
visual_aid.alt_text: GraphQlTester: tests through GraphQL execution engine, not just HTTP

worked_example.scenario: Test Razorpay payment query and merchant mutation.
worked_example.problem_statement:
1. @GraphQlTest(PaymentController.class)
2. Test: payment query with variables
3. Test: mutation createPayment → assert returned id
4. Test: payment not found → error response
5. @MockBean PaymentService

step_by_step_solution.details: @GraphQlTest: loads only the GraphQL layer (controllers, schema, type mappings). Fast: no full Spring context. @MockBean: required for all @Service dependencies. @SpringBootTest: full context. Use @GraphQlTest for GraphQL resolver tests, @SpringBootTest for integration tests that need real DB/services.

things_to_notice: .errors().verify(): must call this OR .errors().satisfy() before path assertions. If you skip it and there IS an error, path assertions will fail with a confusing message instead of 'GraphQL error occurred'.
.path("payments[0].id"): array indexing in the path. Also: .path("payments").entityList(Payment.class) for the full list.

@GraphQlTest vs @SpringBootTest: @GraphQlTest is 5-10× faster (no full Spring context, no DB connections). Use @GraphQlTest for resolver logic tests; @SpringBootTest for integration tests that need real repositories.
.documentName("query"): reads from src/test/resources/graphql-test/query.graphql. Keeps test code clean (no inline GQL strings). Use for complex queries that need variables.

industry_examples.use_case: 200+ @GraphQlTest tests covering: payment queries (filtering, sorting), merchant mutations (create, update), subscription setup, error paths (not found, unauthorized), N+1 prevention (query count assertions with @BatchMapping). Tests run in <30 seconds on CI (vs 3 minutes for full integration tests). Catches schema regressions when resolver field names change.

applications.interview_relevance: 'How do you test a Spring for GraphQL API?' → @GraphQlTest + GraphQlTester. Test slice: only loads GraphQL layer. @MockBean: mock service dependencies. .execute().path('field').entity(Type).isEqualTo(): traverse response. .errors().verify(): assert no errors. Mutation testing: same API, different document. Subscription testing: .executeSubscription().toFlux() + StepVerifier.
applications.real_world_workflow: GraphQL testing workflow: (1) @GraphQlTest(Controller.class) + @MockBean services, (2) create .graphql test documents in src/test/resources/graphql-test/, (3) mock service behavior, (4) execute query/mutation, (5) assert: errors().verify(), then path().entity().isEqualTo(), (6) test error paths: when service throws → errors().satisfy().

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Calling .path().entity() without first calling .errors().verify(). If the response has a GraphQL error, the path will be null or missing — the assertion fails with 'path not found' instead of 'GraphQL error was returned'.
common_gaps.remediation: Always call .errors().verify() before .path() assertions when you expect a successful response. For error scenarios: don't call errors().verify() (it would throw) — use .errors().satisfy() instead to inspect the error details.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Test passes on happy path but gives cryptic 'no value found for path' error instead of 'GraphQL error' when the resolver throws

key_takeaways: @GraphQlTest: fast test slice — only GraphQL layer loaded
.execute().errors().verify(): assert no errors (required before path assertions!)
.errors().satisfy(errors -> ...): inspect error details (for error test cases)

self_check_questions.question: You have a @QueryMapping that should return null when a merchant is not found. How do you test this scenario with GraphQlTester?
self_check_questions.answer: Two possible behaviors: (1) Resolver returns null → GraphQL returns { data: { merchant: null } } (no error). Test: .path('merchant').valueIsNull(). (2) Resolver throws an exception annotated with @GraphQlExceptionHandler or DataFetcherExceptionResolver maps it to a GraphQL error → GraphQL returns { data: null, errors: [...] }. Test: .errors().satisfy(errors -> assertEquals('NOT_FOUND', errors.get(0).getErrorType().toString())). The distinction matters: returning null is NOT the same as throwing an exception in GraphQL. Null = 'field is absent/optional', error = 'something went wrong'. For 'merchant not found': throw an exception that maps to NOT_FOUND error type (better user experience — client knows why it's missing, not just that it's null).

reflection.next_topic_preview: Module 27 GraphQL is complete! You've built queries, mutations, @BatchMapping, subscriptions, and now tests. You have the full GraphQL toolkit for senior Java engineering roles.
